description: A listener follows a quiet voice, and a maker keeps attention on a difficult cut. Both use 'theonu': they direct or hold awareness towards what presently matters. Someone may be 'selua' (aware) without choosing it; 'thesua' (mindful) describes deliberate presence to current experience.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip lightly to the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th', then pass the air into true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Keep stressed rounded true-mid back 'o' in its own syllable across the 'e.o' hiatus. For voiced dental 'n', place the tongue tip behind the upper teeth and lower the velum so air leaves through the nose; finish with high rounded back 'u'.
usage_notes: The thing receiving attention precedes 'theonu', though the verb also works alone. 'sheluo' (listen) directs attention specifically towards sound. English attend an event usually calls for 'pawela' (participate) or a clause of coming or presence; 'theonu' concerns attention. Its noun use covers attending or attention.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Returning to the breath after distraction is another act of 'theonu'. Attention may wander and be directed again.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: When an exchange becomes difficult, 'theonu' keeps attention on the other person's words and response before the next utterance is chosen.

semantic_domains.cognition: awareness deliberately directed or held towards a chosen object
semantic_domains.communication: attention kept on another participant's words or response
